coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots based fluorescent probe and its application for simultaneous, sensitive and selective detection of umami amino acids
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Tingting
Huang, Yang
Gao, Tianyi
Xia, Chunyue
Sun, Chong
Xu, Weimin
Wang, Daoying
coredata.dc:description: In this work, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were synthesized by an efficient one-step hydrothermal using hydrated citric acid and urea as the carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively. The fluorescent quantum yield of the N-GQDs was up to 88.20 %, and the fluorescence lifetime was 12.29 ns. Based on the excellent fluorescence property, water-soluble and low cytotoxicity of N-GQDs, a novel fluorescent probe was fabricated and used for simultaneous detection of l-glutamic acid (l-Glu) and l-aspartic acid (l-Asp). Herein, Al3+ was used as a chelant agent for l-Glu shielding. The sensitive response of the probe towards l-Glu and l-Asp presented a wide concentration range with a low detection limit (S/N = 3). The fluorescent probe showed good selectivity, high stability and acceptable reproducibility in the application of l-Glu/l-Asp simultaneous detection in chicken soup. Meanwhile, the detection results of the fluorescent probe were correlated well with those obtained by amino acid analyzer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent probe
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
Simultaneous detection
l-glutamic acid
l-aspartic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The detection of l-Glu and l-Asp
The mechanism of detection of l-Glu/l-Asp based on N-GQDs
The simultaneous detection of l-Glu and l-Asp
Specificity, reproducibility and stability of the probe

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image, (B) The particles size distributions and (C) UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-GQDs.
(A) FTIR spectrum, (B) Raman spectrum, (C) Survey XPS spectrum; the deconvoluted of C 1s spectra (D), N 1 s spectra (E) and O 1 s spectra (F) of N-GQDs.
CLSM images of MDCK cells incubated with the N-GQDs for different times: (A) 0 h, (B) 1 h and (C) 4 h.

Fluorescence spectra and fluorescence intensity change of N-GQDs with different concentrations (1, 2.5, 5,10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90, 100, 125, 150, 175 and 200 μg/mL) of l-Glu (A-B), l-Asp/Al3+ (C-D), the mixture of l-Asp and l-Glu without Al3+ (E-F), with Al3+ (G-H).
The schematic diagram of the construction of the N-GQDs for the detection of l-Glu and l-Asp in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.0).
Comparison of two methods to determine l-Asp and l-Glu in real samples.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots based fluorescent probe and its application for simultaneous, sensitive and selective detection of umami amino acids

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Agricultural Products Processing, Jiangsu Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Nanjing 210014, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Agricultural Products Processing, Jiangsu Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Nanjing 210014, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Cold Chain Logistics Technology for Agro-product, Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs, Nanjing 210014, PR China

Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Cold Chain Logistics Technology for Agro-product, Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs, 210014, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Suzhou Industrial Park Biotech Development Co., Ltd., Suzhou 215123, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Suzhou Industrial Park Biotech Development Co., Ltd.

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Suzhou Industrial Park Biotech Development Co., Ltd., Suzhou 215123, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Institute of Agricultural Products Processing, Jiangsu Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Nanjing 210014, PR China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were synthesized by an efficient one-step hydrothermal using hydrated citric acid and urea as the carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively. The fluorescent quantum yield of the N-GQDs was up to 88.20 %, and the fluorescence lifetime was 12.29 ns. Based on the excellent fluorescence property, water-soluble and low cytotoxicity of N-GQDs, a novel fluorescent probe was fabricated and used for simultaneous detection of l-glutamic acid (l-Glu) and l-aspartic acid (l-Asp). Herein, Al3+ was used as a chelant agent for l-Glu shielding. The sensitive response of the probe towards l-Glu and l-Asp presented a wide concentration range with a low detection limit (S/N = 3). The fluorescent probe showed good selectivity, high stability and acceptable reproducibility in the application of l-Glu/l-Asp simultaneous detection in chicken soup. Meanwhile, the detection results of the fluorescent probe were correlated well with those obtained by amino acid analyzer.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chicken soup, a good tonic with therapeutic function, has a rich umami taste that makes it palatable and pleasant. Amino acids are one of the important factors for evaluating the nutrition and sensory taste of the soup. Umami taste is mainly caused by a variety of amino acids in chicken, of which l-glutamic acid (l-Glu) and l-Aspartic acid (l-Asp) are the most important flavoring amino acids (Manninen, Rotola-Pukkila, Aisala, Hopia, & Laaksonen, 2018; Zhao, Schieber, & Ganzle, 2016). Moreover, l-Glu and l-Asp have been well-known as important amino acids naturally existing in human metabolism and are found to be vital in learning, memory, movement and brain function (Tabassum et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2011). Thus, the determination of l-Glu and l-Asp are urgently demands. Up to now, various methods have been developed for the detection of amino acids. For example, Ahmed et al. fabricated a sensor based on copper nanoparticles for the spectrophotometry detection of cysteine (Ahmed, Sengan, & Veerappan, 2016). Holzgrabe et al. reported a high performance liquid chromatography method for pharmaceutical quality control of amino acids and organic acids contaminants (Holzgrabe, Nap, & Almeling, 2009). More recently, Yang et al. described a method combining solid-phase extraction and post-column derivatization with gas chromatography-mass spectrometry for the simultaneous detection of free amino acids in drinking water systems (Yang et al., 2019). In industry, the purification and quantification of amino acids were obtained by ion-exchange chromatography and amino acid analyzer (Alpert, Hudecz, & Mechtler, 2015). However, these traditional analytical methods are time-consuming, laborious, which need sophisticated equipment and professional operators (Hsieh et al., 2018; Mentana, Nardiello, Palermo, & Centonze, 2020). Therefore, it is necessary to establish an applicable method known for its simplicity, inexpensiveness and fast response.

Fluorescent probe technology used as an essential research tool for accurately recognizing and standardizing amino acids has been developed (Wang, Liu, Tong, & Ha, 2015; Zhou & Yoon, 2012). Nevertheless, the quantification of l-Glu and l-Asp remains a complicated goal due to high background interference from other amino acids in soup, as well as similar structures between l-Glu and l-Asp, inducing only a small number of probes can discriminate l-Glu and l-Asp (Yuan et al., 2022). Furthermore, due to the extensive use of organic dyes, severe biotoxicity even at relatively low concentrations and other disadvantages, most of the fluorescent probes showed limitations in practical application (Hao, Wang, Wang, Huang, & Cao, 2020). Thus, a simple, rapid and robust method for the simultaneous detection of l-Glu and l-Asp in soup samples is still in urgent need for development.

Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) are a kind of carbon-based zero-dimensional nanomaterial with the advantages of intrinsically fluorescence, low cytotoxicity, water solubility and good biocompatibility (Kaur, Kaur, & Sharma, 2018). Thus, they can be employed as the reporter molecules in biological imaging, photocatalytic reactions, probe construction, drug transportation and so on (Liu, Gong, & Fan, 2016). Hydrothermal method involves dissolving minerals in hot water under high pressure, leading to the formation of single crystals. The fluorescent quantum yield and lifetime are key parameters to evaluate the quality of N-GQDs. A series of N-GQDs has been reported through hydrothermal routes using citric acid and multiple amine as precursor. But these synthesis methods were time-consuming or they need complex post treatment process. Thus, simple approach is still necessary for preparation of N-GQDs with high quantum yield and lifetime. In this work, water-soluble N-GQDs were prepared by a facile, economical and bottom-up hydrothermal method using hydrated citric acid and urea as sources. The N-GQDs exhibited strong fluorescence owing to the abundant surface functional groups and the doping of nitrogen. Due to the presence of high surface negative charges on the surface of N-GQDs, the electrostatic adsorption occurred upon the addition of different concentrations of l-Glu and l-Asp, resulting in the significant fluorescence quenching of N-GQDs. Al3+ was used as a chelant agent for l-Glu shielding, and simultaneous detection of l-Glu and l-Asp was obtained by the change of fluorescence quenching before and after Al3+ addition. More details were presented as follows.

All chemicals were obtained from Aladdin (China) unless otherwise noted. l-Glu (≥99.0 %, HPLC) and l-Asp (≥98.0 %, HPLC) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Urea (≥99.0 %, AR) and hydrated citric acid (≥99.5 %, AR) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., ltd. (China). 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS) consisted of NaCl (≥99.5 %, AR), KCl (≥99.5 %, AR), Na2HPO4 (≥99.0 %, AR) and KH2PO4 (≥99.5 %, AR). All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (18.5 MΩ·cm−1).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were observed by HITACHI H-7650 electron microscopy (Hitachi, Japan) to obtain the morphology and structure of N-GQDs. The size and the zeta potential of the N-GQDs were detected using a Nano Zetasizer (Malvern Panalytical, UK). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was recorded using a Nicolet iS50 infrared spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA) to investigate the surface chemistry and reactivity of N-GQDs. UV–vis spectra were measured by a Cary 5000 UV visible spectrophotometer (Varian, USA) with a slit width of 1 cm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to analyze the surface groups and elemental composition of N-GQDs. Raman spectrum was conducted on a raman spectrometer (HORIBA, USA) to characterize the phase and crystallinity. The microscopy image was taken with an LSM880 laser scanning confocal microscope (ZEISS, Germany). The lifetime of N-GQDs were detected using transient fluorescence spectrometer (HORIBA, USA).

In our experiment, N-GQDs were prepared by a one-step bottom-up hydrothermal method. Firstly, 1.4 g of hydrated citric acid and 1.2 g of urea were dissolved in 100 mL of double-distilled water by stirring for the formation of precursor solution. Then, the obtained solution was hydrothermally treated for 4 h at 160 ℃ in Teflon-lined stainless steel (100 mL). After that, the mixture was dialyzed (MW = 1000 Da) for 24 h and freeze-dried to get the N-CQDs powder for later use. The N-GQDs solution was obtained by dissolving the above power in 0.1 M PBS.

The biocompatibility of N-CQDs was evaluated by cellular imaging of human Hela cells. Briefly, Hela cells were cultured in the medium of RPMI 1640 with 15 % fetal bovine serum. Then, these cells were seeded in a 96-well plate and cell lines were maintained at 37 °C for 24 h containing 5 % CO2. After removing the culture medium and washing cells with PBS, fresh media containing 100 ng/mL of N-GQDs was incubated with Hela cells for 24 h. Subsequently, the excess N-GQDs were eliminated by triple washing with PBS and the fluorescent images were acquired with a laser scanning confocal microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection of l-Glu and l-Asp
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical procedure, the fluorescence spectra were recorded by an LS 55 Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). 20 μg/mL of N-GQDs was taken in the cuvette and the maximum fluorescence intensity was obtained as F0 (Blank) at the excitation wavelength of 345 nm. The N-GQDs were incubated with the same amount of different concentrations of l-Glu for 20 min and the fluorescence intensity change of N-GQDs was recorded in the range of 355 ∼ 600 nm. Then, 10 μg/mL of AlCl3 was added (the volume ratio of AlCl3/N-GQDs was 1/100) to shield the quenching ability of l-Glu to N-GQDs for the formation of Al3+-l-Glu complexes. After that, 500 μL of different concentrations of l-Asp was incubated with AlCl3/N-GQDs for 20 min, thus the relationship between l-Asp and the change of fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs was obtained.

We obtained fresh yellow-feather chickens from Lihua livestock & poultry Co. ltd. (Changzhou, Jiangsu, China). Chickens were electrically stunned for 9 ∼ 12 s using a poultry stunning machine (SQ05, Wujiang Aneng Technology, China) with the voltage of 60 ∼ 80 V, then slaughtered via exsanguination of the left carotid artery. All animal experiments were performed in the Laboratory Animal Center of Jiangsu Academy of Agricultural Sciences and were previously approved by the Department of Science and Technology of Jiangsu Province with the license number of XYSK 2015–0020. Each sample was divided into pieces with each piece of 2 × 2 cm. The optimal sample-water ratio was 1:2 and simmered for 4 h. Then, the above chicken soup was degreased by filtration and vacuum freeze-dried into powder. 1 mg/mL of the practical sample was prepared by dissolving the freeze-dried chicken soup powder with ultrapure water. In addition, an automatic amino acid analyzer was used to determine the content of l-Glu and l-Asp in chicken soup for verifying the accuracy and reliability of the proposed method.

The results were presented as the mean ± SD of triplicate assays. The graphs in this study were made with Origin 8.5 software. The prediction of the total fluorescence intensity change was conducted using IBM SPSS Statistics (version 10, SPSS Inc, Chicago, IL, USA) automatically.

The morphology and size of the N-GQDs were conducted by TEM and Zetasizer, respectively. Fig. 1 A showed the N-GQDs were uniformly dispersed with spherical morphology. And the diameters of the proposed N-GQDs were estimated to be 4.75 ± 0.01 nm (Fig. 1B). In addition, the nanoparticles showed a zeta potential of 3.64 ± 0.12 mV, signifying their good stability with surface positive charges.

Optical property is one of the key factors in evaluating fluorescent material-based sensors. Fig. 1C showed the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the obtained N-GQDs. There were two obvious absorption in the ultraviolet region, and the characteristic peaks were observed at 293 nm and 332 nm, which were related to the typical π → π* transition and n → π* transition, respectively (Leon et al., 2019). Moreover, the digital photographs of N-GQDs were shown in the inset of Fig. 1C. After exposure to normal light, the N-CQDs exhibited light green but emitted a blue color under UV light at 365 nm.

FTIR spectrum was conducted to investigate the surface group of N-GQDs. As illustrated in Fig. 2 A, the peaks located at 3183 and 782 cm−1 revealed the existence of the stretching and wagging vibration of CH bond. Characteristic peaks at 1382 and 1542 cm−1 was related to CN and CC stretching vibration, respectively (Jin, Easton, Tang, Yin, & Hao, 2018; Tan et al., 2018). Meanwhile, the stretching vibration of CH and NH appeared at 2837 and 2992 cm−1 (Siddiqui, Ahmad, Nawaz, Hayat, & Nasir, 2019), which were assigned to the carboxyl group originating from the N-GQD (Liu, Na, Liu, & Su, 2018).

Raman spectroscopy was used to indicate the degree of crystallinity and doping-induced changes in the N-GQDs. As illustrated in Fig. 2B, there was a strong peak at 1306 cm−1 from the carbon atom lattice defects (d-band), while the peak at 1594 cm−1 was ascribed to in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon bonded atoms (G-band) (Rahimi-Aghdam, Shariatinia, Hakkarainen, & Haddadi-Asl, 2020). Furthermore, the peaks at 1232 and 2132 cm−1 were related to CNC and CN, respectively (Zhang et al., 2015), indicating nitrogen was doped into N-GQDs successfully.

To further characterize the elemental composition of N-GQDs and comprehend the bonding configuration of nitrogen atoms in N-GQDs, the survey XPS spectrum and the deconvoluted of C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s spectra were studied. As shown in Fig. 2C, there were three peaks that appeared at 285.2, 400.4 and 533.0 eV, which were ascribed to C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s, respectively. The peak-fitting of C 1 s shown in Fig. 2D could be decomposed into three dominant peaks including CC at 284.5 eV, CO at 286.7 eV and O-CO at 288.6 eV, respectively (Boas et al., 2019; Bulusheva et al., 2020). According to the N 1 s spectra (Fig. 2E), there were two single peaks that appeared at 400.2 and 401.5 eV. The peak at 400.2 eV was caused by NH and the peak at 401.5 eV was assigned to pyridine N atoms doped in the structure of N-GQDs (Liu et al., 2020; Zhou, Sui, Liu, Wang, & Han, 2019). These results further confirmed N element was doped in the as-prepared N-GQDs. Meanwhile, the O 1 s spectra of N-GQDs shown in Fig. 2F exhibited three peaks, namely CO, CO and C–OH with the corresponding binding energy values of 532.0, 533.1 and 533.7 eV, respectively (Ju & Chen, 2015). Based on these, numerous functional groups containing nitrogen, oxygen and carbon presented in N-GQDs, leading to a good solubility and stability of N-GQDs in aqueous media.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of detection of l-Glu/l-Asp based on N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The simultaneous detection mechanism of l-Glu/l-Asp by the N-GQDs based fluorescent probe was explored in Scheme 1 . The N-GQDs emitted strong blue fluorescence under the excitation of 345 nm due to the successful doping of nitrogen and the chromophoric/auxochromic groups on the surface of N-GQDs (Zhu et al., 2013). l-Asp and l-Glu were negatively charged in a neutral environment (chemical structural see Fig. S3). After incubation with the N-GQDs, electrostatic adsorption occurred between the two amino acids and the positively charged N-GQDs, resulting in a quenched fluorescence behavior (Gao et al., 2022). In the theory of fluorescence quenching, the distance between the quencher and the fluorophore is a determining factor for the quenching effect (Wu, Xing, Zeng, Huber, & Zhao, 2018). The distance between the generated coordination complex as a quencher and the N-GQDs as a fluorophore was larger since the carbon chain length of l-Glu was longer than that of l-Asp, resulting in a weakened quenching efficiency of N-GQDs by l-Glu. Meanwhile, the binding constant between N-GQDs and l-Asp, l-Glu was investigated by fluorescence spectroscopy (the detailed measurement process see Electronic Supplementary Information ). The results indicated that the combination reactions of N-GQDs with l-Asp and l-Glu were static quenching process (Fig. S4∼S6, Table S1∼S3). Furtherly, it was proved that N-GQDs and l-Asp have stronger affinity than that of N-GQDs/l-Glu. Al3+ is a kind of Lewis acid with a high positive oxidation state that can coordinate with carboxyl and carbonyl groups. According to Mujika et al., l-Glu was coordinated tridentatedly to Al (III) is clearly presented, which was the most stable structure in solution (Mujika, Ugalde, & Lopez, 2014). Meanwhile, at the same temperature, the competition coordination of Al3+ and N-GQDs was weaker than that of l-Asp and l-Glu. Bases on these, in presence of an appropriate concentration of Al3+, the quenching ability of l-Glu on N-GQDs was nearly eliminated, while l-Asp could maintain the quenching effect on the N-GQDs because l-Glu possessed a higher ability to donate electrons or interact with the empty d-orbitals of the Al3+ than l-Asp (Deschaume et al., 2017). Therefore, the simultaneous detection of l-Glu and l-Asp was obtained by the change of PL quenching before and after the addition of Al3+.

The fluorescence emission spectra shown in Fig. S7 indicated that N-GQDs emitted good luminescence (curve a), and the fluorescence intensity decreased obviously with the addition of l-Glu and l-Asp. The quenching ability of l-Glu to N-GQDs (curve b) was stronger than that of l-Asp (curve c) at the equivalent concentration. The addition of Al3+ was used to shield the quenching ability of l-Glu on N-GQDs due to the combination of l-Glu and Al3+ (curve d), while l-Asp had the identical quenching ability for N-GQDs (curve e) compared to the absence of Al3+. The results proved the complex of the l-Glu-Al3+ was stable, which was conducive to eliminate the quenching ability of l-Glu on N-GQDs.

To study the cellular uptake of N-GQDs, the fluorescence imaging with the incubation time was measured by confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM). As shown in Fig. 3 A, Madin-Darby canine kidney (MDCK) cells showed almost no fluorescent signal at 345 nm. After incubated with the N-GQDs for 1 h, blue fluorescence was obtained inside the cells under ultraviolet excitation wavelengths at 345 nm (Fig. 3B). While incubated with the N-GQDs for 4 h, blue fluorescence enhanced, indicating the efficient cellular uptake of N-GQDs (Fig. 3C). The results suggested that the N-GQDs as a fluorescent probe efficiently penetrated into MDCK cells via internalization and marked successfully without significant damage to these cell (Wang, Qi, Yang, Wang, & Xu, 2021).

Hemolysis assay were also used to evaluate the biocompatibility of N-CQDs. Only 3.69 ± 0.32 % hemolysis rate were obtained (Fig. S8), indicating the N-CQDs had a negligible effect on hemolysis (<5%) on RBCs comparing with the negative control (PBS). The results indicated that the N-GQDs had low cytotoxicity, which made it possible candidates for fluorescent probe as well as evaluation of food quality.

In order to improve the performance of the fluorescent probe, the excitation wavelength, pH and incubation time were investigated (Fig. S9). More details were discussed in Electronic Supplementary Information (Tabaraki & Nateghi, 2016).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The simultaneous detection of l-Glu and l-Asp
ce:section.ce:para: In the presence of l-Glu or l-Asp, the N-GQDs probe interact electrostatically with the target amino-acid molecules, which resulted in a quenched fluorescence behavior and increased the fluorescence intensity change of the N-GQDs based probe. Such a signal change (ΔF) correlated to the level of analytes. The higher the ΔF value was, the larger number of analytes bound to the N-GQDs probes was. The original fluorescence intensity of the N-GQDs probe was deemed F0, and the reduction fluorescence intensity was recorded as F. The signal change was determined by the following equation: ΔF= |F − F0|. Empirical study is divided into three steps. Firstly, the specific detection of l-Glu/l-Asp was carried out by determining the fluorescence intensity change of N-GQDs incubated with different concentrations of amino-acid molecules, and the relationships were presented in Fig. 4 A-D. It could be seen that the fluorescence intensity change of N-GQDs gradually decreased with the increasing concentration of l-Glu in the range of 0 ∼ 200 μg/mL (Fig. 4A). Under the optimal experimental conditions, the linear relationships between the fluorescence intensity change of N-GQDs and the concentration of l-Glu was: ΔF1 = 197.26 ln c–366.60 (Equation 1, Fig. 4B) with the detection limit of 2.22 μg/mL (R2 = 0.994, S/N = 3). Meantime, l-Asp had the identical quenching ability for N-GQDs with/without Al3+, which was proved by 3.2 The mechanism of detection of l-Glu/l-Asp based on N-GQDs. Fig. 4C-D demonstrated that with addition of Al3+, the calibration curve of l-Asp was ΔF2 = 179.96 ln c–251.84 (Equation 2) with the detection limit of 2.78 μg/mL (R2 = 0.996, S/N = 3) in the range of 0 ∼ 200 μg/mL. Moreover, the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity change of N-GQDs and the total concentration of l-Glu and l-Asp without Al3+ was: ΔFTotal = 199.94 ln c–149.78 with the detection limit of 2.18 μg/mL (R2 = 0.9912, S/N = 3, Fig. 4E-F). From Fig. 4E-F, the simultaneous detection of l-Glu and l-Asp could not be obtained by the fabricated fluorescent probe. Then, 10 μg/mL of Al3+ was added and used as a masking reagent in the mixture of l-Glu and l-Asp. The formation of the complex between Al3+ and l-Glu was the cause of the anti-quenching ability of l-Glu to the N-GQDs through decreasing the interaction between l-Glu and N-GQDs. However, the quenching function of l-Asp could preserve for the interaction with the N-GQDs. The relationship between the fluorescence intensity change of N-CQDs in the mixture of l-Glu and l-Asp with addition of Al3+ was ΔF = 122.81 ln c–165.56 (Fig. 4G-H) with the detection limit of 1.73 μg/mL (R2 = 0.9847, S/N = 3). In practical samples, the proportion of l-Glu and l-Asp varied, and it is necessary to explore the relationship between the effect of l-Glu (ΔF1), l-Asp (ΔF2) and the mixture of l-Glu and l-Asp (ΔFtotal) on the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs with addition of Al3+. Thus, different concentrations of l-Glu, l-Asp and the mixtures of l-Glu and l-Asp on the fluorescence intensity change of N-GQDs were measured and exhibited in Table S7. The relationship between ΔF1, ΔF2 and ΔFtotal was analyzed by IBM SPSS Statistics, and the empirical equation (Equation S1) was obtained as following: ln (ΔFtotal) = 0.44 ln (ΔF1) + 0.025 ln (ΔF2) + 3.44 (R2 = 0.96). According to the given ΔF2, the concentrations of l-Asp could be calculated by Equation 2. Based on ΔFtotal and ΔF2, the fluorescence intensity change quenched by l-Glu (ΔF1) could calculated by Equation S1, and the concentration of l-Glu in the mixed samples could be obtained by Equation 1. Thus, the simultaneous detection of l-Glu and l-Asp was achieved by measuring the fluorescence intensity change before and after Al3+ addition without the process of separation. As listed in Table S8, compared with other reported methods (Gholami, Farjami, & Ghasemi, 2021; Jamal et al., 2018; Jamal, Hasan, Mathewson, & Razeeb, 2013; Liang et al., 2015), the simultaneous detection of l-Glu and l-Asp by this fluorescent probe could be obtained with low detection limit, wide linear range and fine simplicity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity, reproducibility and stability of the probe
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the specificity of the fabricated fluorescent probe, the effect of different kinds of amino acids on the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs based probe was explored at the concentration of 100 μg/mL. In addition, the interference of other nutrients, such as proteins, metal ions, etc. was detected, since these also existed in chicken soup. The results exhibited that obvious fluorescence quenching of N-GQDs could be observed by l-Glu and l-Asp, while the addition of the other substances including amino acids, proteins, cations, etc. resulted in an intact or slightly lowered fluorescence signal (Fig. S10A-B). Thus, the probe featured an excellent selectivity for l-Glu and l-Asp detection. The reproducibility of the probe was investigated by testing ten parallel experiments, and the relative standard deviation was 2.86 %. Meanwhile, the long-term stability of the probe was also studied. The fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs remained 96.78 % of the initial value after being stored at 4 ℃ for ten days, suggesting that the proposed fluorescent probe possessed acceptable stability.

In order to illustrate the feasibility of the proposed fluorescent probes, amino acid analyzer (Hitachi, Japan) was used as a comparative method to detect l-Glu and l-Asp in chicken soup. As shown in Table 1 , the detection results of the fluorescent probe were consistent with the data detected by the amino acid analyzer, indicating that the fluorescent probe could be used for the simultaneous detection of l-Glu and l-Asp in practical samples.

In summary, we established the N-GQDs based fluorescent probe for the simultaneous detection of l-Glu and l-Asp. The proposed fluorescent probe exhibited several advantages. Firstly, the N-GQDs were synthesized via a convenient one-step hydrothermal method which showed unique fluorescent luminescence properties for abundant surface functional groups. Secondly, the proposed fluorescent probe exhibited high selectivity, which could be used for the specific detection of l-Glu and l-Asp. In the presence of Al3+, the simultaneous detection of l-Glu and l-Asp was obtained. Moreover, the detection results in actual samples were consistent with the amino acid analyzer, indicating that the fluorescent probe could be used as a promising platform for the evaluation of umami amino acids.

Tingting Han: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Writing – original draft. Yang Huang: Validation, Data curation. Tianyi Gao: Investigation. Chunyue Xia: Investigation. Chong Sun: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Weimin Xu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Daoying Wang: Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by Jiangsu Agriculture Science and Technology Innovation Fund (No. CX(21)3042), Natural Science Foundation Program of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20211141) and China Agriculture Research System (NO. CARS-41).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2022.134509.
